summary: Jeff Atwood uses the now-obsolete PC turbo button as a metaphor for the importance of speed in software development. He argues that just as you'd always want your hardware running at maximum speed, the same philosophy applies to software development workflows. Drawing on historical examples from the evolution of development environments—from batch processing mainframes to Turbo Pascal—he demonstrates how faster feedback loops dramatically increase programmer productivity. The post concludes by challenging readers to identify what's preventing them from working at maximum speed.
key_points: The turbo button metaphor illustrates that performance should always be maximized, not held in reserve
Fast iteration cycles are essential for developer productivity—from hardware to development tools to deployment practices
Historical progression from once-per-day batch compiles to instant feedback fundamentally changed what programmers could accomplish
Fred Brooks' research suggested interactive programming facilities at least double productivity in system programming
Speed improvements benefit both developers (faster build times, debugging) and users (responsive software)
money_quotes: The strange thing about a turbo button is that you'd pretty much always want it on; there's almost no situation where you'd want to keep some power in reserve for that extra 'oomph' required by, say, a particularly intensive Lotus 1-2-3 spreadsheet.
Then I got Turbo Pascal and life was forever changed. I could write, compile and debug applications virtually instantly and my need for speed has never looked back.
Still nothing was ever as instant as Turbo had been, despite the hardware being 100x faster.
As far as I'm concerned, everything on a PC should happen instantaneously. Or at least as close to instantaneous as the laws of physics will allow.
Simply doing everything faster, all other things being equal, will allow you to get more done.
Harr's data suggest that an interactive facility at least doubles productivity in system programming.
Never underestimate the power of pressing the software turbo button.
key_insight: Developer productivity is fundamentally tied to the speed of feedback loops—faster tools and shorter iteration cycles compound into dramatically more effective software development.
